zh.lifeSummary: 當Elvira Ponce de Minerva y Trava於大約0995年出生在西班牙卡塔倫亞赫羅納瑞爾斯福格雷斯 德 蒙左利時，她的父親Ponce de Minerva-133歲，母親Condesa Estefania Ramirez de Cifuentes-168歲。她於1020年與Don Rodrigo de Sanchez El Velloso Señor de Cabrera y de Ribera在葡萄牙可布拉可布拉結婚。他們至少是2個兒子和1個女兒的父母。她去世在西班牙拉 利奧亞納拉。

nameMeanings.name: Ponce
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Manuel, Juan, Luis, Jesus, Carlos, Francisco, Jorge, Miguel, Pedro, Raul, Roberto.

Spanish and French: from the medieval personal name Ponce, ultimately from Pontius, a Roman surname, perhaps an ethnic name for someone from Pontus (named from Greek pontos ‘ocean’) in Asia Minor, or an Italic cognate of Latin Quintus ‘fifth’ (i.e. ‘fifth-born’). The name was borne by two 3rd-century Christian saints, a Carthaginian deacon and a martyr of Nice, but was not widely popular in the Middle Ages because of the inhibiting influence of the even more famous Pontius Pilate. In some cases though, the surname may have been originally used for someone who had played the part of this character in a religious play.

variants.name: Ponce de Leon
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Luis, Cesar, Mercedes, Alberto, Francisco, Javier, Juan, Lazaro, Leticia, Manuel, Ramon.
Spanish (Ponce de León): compound of the surname Ponce + the habitational name León (see Leon ).

History: Juan Ponce De León, the Spanish explorer who discovered Florida, came from a noble Aragonese family. He is believed to have been on board for Columbus's second voyage to Hispaniola in 1493. After his death in Florida from a wound from an Indian arrow in 1521, Ponce De León's title and rights in Florida were passed on to his son.

variants.name: Points
meanings: English (of Norman origin):
from the Old French personal name Ponz, Ponce, from Latin Pontius (see Ponce ).

habitational name from any of several places called Ponts (such as those in Manche and in Seine-Maritime, Normandy), from Latin pontes ‘bridges’. This name has been established since the 13th century in Ireland, where it appears as Pounce and Pounche, and is Gaelicized as Púinse.

variants.name: Pons
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Juan, Pedro, Ramon, Guillermo, Roberto, Carlos, Francisco, Luis, Mario, Rafael, Apolonia.  French Adrien, Armand, Emile, Jacques, Marcel, Marcelle, Matilde.
Catalan, French (Occitan), and Dutch: from the medieval personal name Pons (see Ponce ).
German: patronymic from a short form of an ancient Germanic personal name, such as Bonhard, formed with the element bon ‘request, petition’ (compare Latin bonus).

variants.name: Ponzio
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Vito, Carlo, Carmine, Leonardo, Pasquale, Rocco, Salvatore, Silvio, Vincenzo.
Italian: from the personal name Ponzio, from Latin Pontius (see Ponce ).

variants.name: Poncelet
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Alain, Benoit.  German Erwin, Fritz.
French (mainly northeastern) and Walloon:
topographic name from a diminutive of Old French poncel ‘little bridge’, or a habitational name from (Le) Poncelet, the name of several places in the northeastern part of France.

variants.name: Poinsett
meanings: Altered form of French Poinset: from a pet form of the personal name Poinse, from Latin Pontius (see Ponce ). Altered ending reflects the Canadian and American French practice of sounding the final -t. The surname Poinset is very rare in France.

History: The poinsettia takes its name from an American diplomat, Joel Poinsett (1779–1851), who was a keen amateur botanist and who brought the flower back from Mexico, where he was US ambassador. He was born in Charleston, SC, and was descended from a Huguenot, Pierre Poinset, who was born in Soubise in Charente-Maritime and settled in Charleston c. 1700. Pierre Poinset is listed in the (US) National Huguenot Society's register of qualified Huguenot ancestors and also in the similar registers of the Huguenot Society of America and of the Huguenot Society of South Carolina.

variants.name: Point
meanings: French: from the personal name Point, from Latin Pontius (see Ponce ).
English: variant of Points with loss of final -s. This surname is rare in Britain.

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Minerva
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Vito, Domenic, Angelo, Giuseppe, Italia, Salvatore.
Italian: from the female personal name Minerva, from thename of the Roman goddess of wisdom, corresponding to GreekAthena.

nameMeanings.name: Trava
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Francesco, Gasper, Antonio, Armando, Biagio, Emanuele, Gustavo, Marco, Nicola, Santo.
Italian (southern): perhaps a nickname from trama ‘weft’, ‘plot’.

nameMeanings.name: Elvira
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: name of Germanic (Visigothic) origin , very common in the Middle Ages. It was not used in the English-speaking world until the 19th century, when it was made familiar as the name of the long-suffering wife of  Don Juan , both in  Mozart's  opera Don Giovanni (  1789 ) and in  Byron's  satirical epic poem Don Juan (  1819–24 ). In his comedy Blithe Spirit  Noel Coward  used it as the name of  Charles Condomine's  first wife, who returns to haunt him. It is also the name of the heroine of the Swedish film Elvira Madigan (  1967 ), directed by  Bo Widerberg , a romantic tragedy about a pair of lovers who would rather die than be separated.

en.lifeSummary: When Elvira Ponce de Minerva y Trava was born about 0995, in Fogueres de Montsoriu, Riells, Girona, Catalonia, Spain, her father, Ponce de Minerva, was -133 and her mother, Condesa Estefania Ramirez de Cifuentes, was -168. She married Don Rodrigo de Sanchez El Velloso Señor de Cabrera y de Ribera in 1020, in Coimbra, Coimbra, Portugal. They were the parents of at least 2 sons and 1 daughter. She died in Nájera, La Rioja, Spain.
